section.title: The LORD's Anger Against Israel
section.sectionRationale: The judgment oracles reach their most severe pitch. Ephraim, once exalted, died through Baal; prosperity bred pride and forgetfulness, so God will meet them like a lion and a bear. Yet even at the darkest, a startling promise flashes out: I will ransom them from the power of Sheol, the verse Paul takes up in the triumph over death.
section.thesis: Because Israel forgot the God who alone is Savior and turned to idols and kings, judgment falls like a wild beast; yet the same God who declares there is no Savior besides Me announces He will ransom His people from the power of death.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the idols that brought death and God the only Savior; the pride of the well-fed and the beast of judgment; and the ransom from Sheol shining amid a final word of wrath on Samaria.

units.label: Idols that bring death, a Savior forgotten
units.note: no God and no Savior besides Me

verses.text: When Ephraim spoke, there was trembling; he was exalted in Israel. But he incurred guilt through Baal, and he died.

Now they sin more and more and make for themselves cast images, idols skillfully made from their silver, all of them the work of craftsmen. People say of them, “They offer human sacrifice and kiss the calves!”
Therefore they will be like the morning mist, like the early dew that vanishes, like chaff blown from a threshing floor, like smoke through an open window.
Yet I am the LORD your God ever since the land of Egypt; you know no God but Me, for there is no {{yasha|Savior}} besides Me.

units.label: Pride of the well-fed, the beast of judgment

verses.text: I knew you in the wilderness, in the land of drought.
When they had pasture, they became satisfied; when they were satisfied, their hearts became proud, and as a result they forgot Me.
So like a lion I will pounce on them; like a leopard I will lurk by the path.
Like a bear robbed of her cubs I will attack them, and I will tear open their chests. There I will devour them like a lion, like a wild beast tearing them apart.

units.label: Ransomed from the power of death
units.note: where, O Death, is your sting?

verses.text: You are destroyed, O Israel, because you are against Me, against your helper.
Where is your king now to save you in all your cities, and the rulers to whom you said, “Give me a king and princes”?
So in My anger I gave you a king, and in My wrath I took him away.
The iniquity of Ephraim is bound up; his sin is stored up.
Labor pains come upon him, but he is an unwise son. When the time arrives, he fails to present himself at the opening of the womb.

I will {{padah|ransom}} them from the power of {{sheol|Sheol}}; I will {{gaal|redeem}} them from Death. Where, O Death, are your plagues? Where, O Sheol, is your sting? Compassion is hidden from My eyes.

Although he flourishes among his brothers, an east wind will come, a wind from the LORD rising up from the desert. His fountain will fail, and his spring will run dry. The wind will plunder his treasury of every precious article.
Samaria will bear her guilt because she has rebelled against her God. They will fall by the sword; their little ones will be dashed to pieces, and their pregnant women ripped open.

yasha.gloss: To save, to deliver, to rescue; the root behind the words for Savior and salvation, and behind the name Jesus, the LORD saves. In verse 4 God insists there is no Savior besides Me, exposing the folly of trusting idols and kings, and pointing to the salvation He alone gives, fulfilled in Christ.

padah.gloss: To ransom, to redeem by paying a price, to buy back. In verse 14 God promises to ransom His people from the power of Sheol, a stunning pledge of rescue from death itself, taken up by Paul in the triumph of resurrection over the grave.

sheol.gloss: The grave, the realm of the dead, the pit. It pictures the power of death that swallows all people. God's promise to ransom His people from the power of Sheol declares His mastery over death, the very power Christ breaks in His resurrection.

gaal.gloss: To redeem, to act as kinsman-redeemer, to buy back what is lost and reclaim one's own. Alongside padah in verse 14, gaal stresses that God rescues His people as their near kinsman, a role fulfilled when Christ becomes our brother and redeems us from Death itself.

basic.context: This is the sternest chapter in Hosea. Ephraim was once honored, but idolatry killed it spiritually. The people keep making silver idols and even kiss calf images. God reminds them that He alone has been their God since Egypt, and there is no Savior besides Him. But when they grew full and comfortable, they became proud and forgot Him, so He will come against them like a lion, a leopard, and a bear. Their kings cannot save them. And yet, in the middle of the darkness, God flashes out a promise: I will ransom them from the power of the grave, and He taunts Death itself.
basic.hermeneutics: The chapter's severity is real covenant judgment, climaxing in the horror of verse 16. Verse 14 is famously double-edged in Hebrew and can be read as promise or as taunt; Paul settles its ultimate meaning by quoting it as the shout of resurrection triumph. Read canonically, the ransom from death is God's last word over His people, even though wrath falls in history.
basic.originalLanguages: God declares there is no yasha, Savior, besides Him, the root behind the name Jesus. Then, astonishingly, He promises to padah, ransom, and gaal, redeem, His people from sheol, the grave, and from Death. Savior, ransom, kinsman-redeemer, and the grave defeated: this is the vocabulary of the gospel breaking into the darkest oracle.
basic.typology: God as the only Savior points directly to Jesus, whose name means the LORD saves. The promise to ransom and redeem from Sheol and Death is fulfilled in Christ's resurrection, which Paul proclaims by quoting this very verse. The unwise son who will not come forth from the womb points by contrast to Christ, the wise Son who comes forth from the tomb in resurrection life.
basic.christ: God says there is no Savior besides Me, and the New Testament names that Savior Jesus, the LORD who saves. The most electrifying line, I will ransom them from the power of Sheol, I will redeem them from Death, is taken up by Paul in 1 Corinthians 15, where the taunt Where, O Death, is your sting becomes the church's resurrection song. What Hosea announces God will do, Christ accomplishes: by His own death and rising He ransoms His people from the grave and disarms Death forever. The wrath that falls in this chapter is real, and Christ absorbs it, so that the promise of ransom stands as God's final word.
basic.god: God is the sole Savior, and this chapter shows how jealously He guards that truth, for idols and kings only bring death. He is holy and just; forgetfulness bred by comfort provokes His fierce anger, pictured as a wounded, predatory beast. Yet at the same moment He reveals a mercy that reaches past the grave itself, promising to ransom His people from death. His justice and His saving love meet here in tension that only the cross and empty tomb resolve.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Corinthians 15:55 quotes Hosea's taunt over death in the triumph of resurrection. Matthew 1:21 names Jesus, for He will save His people. Acts 4:12 declares there is salvation in no one else.
basic.oneStory: Chapter 13 shows a people killed by idolatry and pride, facing the beast of judgment, yet hearing the promise of ransom from the grave. The Bible answers this in Christ, the only Savior, who by His resurrection ransoms His people from Sheol and turns the taunt of Death into the shout of victory.

context.literary: The most intense of the judgment oracles, dense with predatory imagery, lion, leopard, bear, and with the vanishing images of mist, dew, chaff, and smoke. Its structure drives from idolatry through pride to judgment, but is pierced at its center by the ransom promise of verse 14.
context.historical: The making of silver idols and kissing of calves reflects the entrenched cult of the northern kingdom. The demand give me a king and the taking away of kings recall Israel's original request in Samuel and the collapse of its monarchy. The east wind and the fall of Samaria describe the Assyrian conquest that ended the kingdom.
context.canonical: Verse 14 is quoted by Paul in 1 Corinthians 15:55 as the church's cry of resurrection victory, making this chapter a crucial Old Testament root of the New Testament hope. The insistence that God alone is Savior threads through Isaiah and into the New Testament confession of Christ as the only Savior.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To confront Israel with the deadliness of idolatry and the pride bred by comfort, to insist that God alone is Savior, and to hold out, even amid severe judgment, the promise that God can ransom His people from death itself.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes Israel's sin and the resulting judgment, including the atrocities of war in verse 16, which it laments rather than commends. What endures is the truth that God alone saves and the hope of ransom from death fulfilled in Christ.

debates.question: Is verse 14 a promise of ransom or a taunt of coming judgment?

views.label: A promise of ransom from death
views.case: Read as a pledge, God vows to ransom and redeem His people from Sheol and Death, and Paul confirms this reading by quoting it as resurrection triumph in 1 Corinthians 15:55.

views.label: A taunt summoning death's plagues
views.case: In context, with compassion hidden from My eyes, some read it as God summoning Death's plagues against Israel, so that the questions call for death to do its worst in judgment.

debates.bearing: The Hebrew holds both edges, but the New Testament gives the decisive word: in Christ the verse becomes the gospel's shout of victory, so that the ultimate meaning is ransom from the grave, even where judgment falls first in history.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's key claim is that there is no yasha, Savior, besides the LORD, the very root that gives the name Jesus its meaning, the LORD saves. Against that backdrop the darkest oracle in Hosea produces its brightest flash: God will padah, ransom, and gaal, redeem, His people from sheol, the grave, and from Death. Padah pictures rescue by paying a price; gaal pictures a kinsman reclaiming his own; sheol is the realm of the dead that swallows all. The Hebrew of verse 14 shimmers between promise and taunt, but Paul fixes its meaning by making it the church's resurrection cry, Where, O Death, is your sting.

typology.type: The LORD who alone is Savior
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, whose name means the LORD saves, the only Savior of the world, Matthew 1:21 and Acts 4:12.

typology.type: The promise to ransom and redeem from Sheol and Death
typology.fulfillment: Christ's resurrection, by which He ransoms His people from the grave, 1 Corinthians 15:55.

typology.type: The unwise son who will not come forth from the womb
typology.fulfillment: Christ the wise Son who comes forth from the tomb in resurrection life, Colossians 1:18.

study.christ: God insists there is no Savior besides Him, and the New Testament reveals that Savior in Jesus, the LORD who saves. The verse that pierces the gloom, I will ransom them from the power of Sheol, I will redeem them from Death, becomes in Paul's hands the church's song of triumph: Where, O Death, is your sting? What Hosea announces God will do, Christ accomplishes in fact, ransoming His people from the grave by His own death and resurrection and stripping Death of its power. The fierce wrath of this chapter is not denied; Christ bears it fully, so that the promise of ransom is not cancelled by judgment but secured through it. The unwise son who could not come forth from the womb is answered by the wise Son who comes forth from the tomb, the firstborn from the dead.
study.god: The LORD reveals Himself as the one and only Savior, and this chapter shows how deadly it is to look elsewhere, for idols and kings deliver only death. He is holy and rightly angered by a people whose comfort made them forget Him, and His judgment is pictured with terrifying force. Yet in the same oracle He unveils a mercy that reaches past the grave, promising to ransom His people from death itself. Here God's justice and His saving love stand together in a tension too great for the Old Testament to resolve on its own, awaiting the cross and the empty tomb where both are fully honored.

crossReferences.note: Paul quotes Hosea's taunt over death as the church's resurrection victory in Christ.
You shall call His name Jesus, for He will save His people, fulfilling the LORD is the only Savior.
There is salvation in no one else, echoing there is no Savior besides Me.
Christ is risen, the firstfruits, the wise Son who comes forth from the tomb.

study.oneStory: Chapter 13 shows a people killed by idolatry and pride, facing the beast of judgment, yet hearing the astonishing promise of ransom from the grave. The gospel answers this in Christ, the only Savior, who by His resurrection ransoms His people from Sheol and turns the taunt of Death into the shout of victory.

quiz.q: What does God insist about Himself in verse 4?

options.text: He alone is God and Savior; there is no Savior besides Him.
options.note: Verse 4 declares there is no Savior besides the LORD.

options.text: That idols can save if made well enough.
options.note: The chapter exposes idols as bringing death, not salvation.

options.text: That kings are the true saviors of Israel.
options.note: Their kings cannot save them, verse 10.

options.text: That salvation comes from Assyria.
options.note: God alone is Savior; Assyria brings judgment.

quiz.q: What made Israel forget God, according to verse 6?

options.text: When they were satisfied and full, their hearts became proud and they forgot Him.
options.note: Comfort bred pride and forgetfulness, verse 6.

options.text: Severe famine and hardship.
options.note: It was fullness, not famine, that led them astray here.

options.text: Being conquered too early.
options.note: The forgetting came in prosperity, before the conquest.

options.text: Never having known God at all.
options.note: God knew them in the wilderness; they forgot a God they knew.

quiz.q: How does Paul use verse 14 in the New Testament?

options.text: He quotes Where, O Death, is your sting as the church's cry of resurrection victory in Christ.
options.note: 1 Corinthians 15:55 makes it the shout of triumph over death.

options.text: He never refers to it.
options.note: Paul quotes it directly in 1 Corinthians 15.

options.text: He uses it to teach that death always wins.
options.note: He uses it to proclaim death defeated in Christ.

options.text: He uses it to forbid mourning.
options.note: He uses it to celebrate resurrection victory over death.

quiz.q: How does the promise to ransom from Sheol point to Christ?

options.text: It is fulfilled in Christ's resurrection, by which He ransoms His people from the grave and disarms death.
options.note: Christ accomplishes the ransom Hosea announces, 1 Corinthians 15.

options.text: It means no believer ever physically dies.
options.note: It means death is defeated and cannot hold God's people, not that they never die.

options.text: It refers only to escaping the Assyrians.
options.note: The language reaches past political rescue to victory over death itself.

options.text: It has no bearing on Christ.
options.note: Paul ties it directly to Christ's resurrection.

quiz.q: How do God's severe wrath and His ransom promise fit together?

options.text: Both are real, and they are finally reconciled at the cross and empty tomb, where justice and saving love meet.
options.note: The tension of chapter 13 is resolved in Christ.

options.text: The wrath cancels the promise entirely.
options.note: Paul shows the ransom promise stands and triumphs in Christ.

options.text: The promise proves the wrath was never real.
options.note: The wrath is real; Christ bears it so the promise stands.

options.text: They cannot both be true in any sense.
options.note: Both are true and meet in the crucified and risen Christ.

questions: Comfort and fullness led Israel to forget God. How might your own seasons of ease quietly dull your dependence on the Lord?
God insists there is no Savior besides Him. What lesser saviors, whether people, plans, or possessions, are you tempted to trust for rescue?
In the darkest chapter God promises to ransom His people from the grave. How does the hope of resurrection change the way you face death and loss?
Paul turned verse 14 into a shout of victory over death. How can you make Where, O Death, is your sting a real song of hope in your own trials?
Israel's idols brought death, but Christ brings life. What idol do you need to lay down in order to cling to the only Savior?

translationNotes.note: They offer human sacrifice and kiss the calves may also be rendered the men who sacrifice kiss the calves, compare 1 Kings 19:18.
Where is your king now may also be rendered Shall I be your King now, a play on Israel's demand for a human king.

The Hebrew can be read as promise or as a taunt summoning judgment; the Septuagint reads Where, O Death, is your penalty? Where, O Hades, is your sting? and this is cited in 1 Corinthians 15:55 as resurrection victory. The BSB em dashes in this and surrounding verses are rendered here as commas per house style.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Hosea 13:1-16, with poetic lines joined into running verses. Em dashes in the source have been rendered as commas per house style.
